EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds in Estes Park caused a roof under construction at the YMCA of the Rockies to collapse.  The wind caught the end section of the roof truss, and in a domino effect, blew it into the second and thirds trusses, on down the line of the roof.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two snowmobilers were killed on the side of Gravel Mountain near Grand Lake.  The avalanche occurred when one of the men tried to assist the other, after his snowmobile had become stuck in the snow.  Both men were buried by the slide and died at the scene of the accident.  Another snowmobiler was partially buried in the avalanche, but was not injured.

Another brief period of high winds occurred in and near the foothills of Boulder and Jefferson Counties.  In Nederland, the strong wind snapped a blue spruce which landed on a nearby propane tank.  Some roofs in the immediate area were damaged.  Power lines were also downed; which left 126 residence without electricity for about 6 hours. Peak wind gusts included 90 mph at the National Wind Technology Center, and 89 mph, 6 miles northwest of Boulder.

CZ_NAME: WEST JACKSON & WEST GRAND COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jetstream brought very heavy snow to portions of the North-Central Mountains.  The Park and Medicine Bow Ranges received most of the heavy snow, with storm totals of 24 inches at Tower and 16 inches at the Columbine Snotel.  Storm totals elsewhere generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches.  Strong winds also accompanied the snow.  Peak gusts included:  71 mph at Mines Peak, 67 mph at Niwot Ridge, and 60 mph atop Wilkerson Pass.

Heavy rain caused flash flooding at the intersection of State Highway 71 and County Road T.  The floodwaters, up to a foot in depth, were reportedly flowing across several county roads south of Punkin Center and west of Karval. Flooding was observed along County Road T all the way from State Highway 71 to just east of Karval.

CZ_NAME: WEST JACKSON & WEST GRAND COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow and blowing snow in the North-Central Mountains, and strong downslope winds in the Front Range Foothills, continued to make travel into the high country difficult.  Storm totals included:  10 inches at the Eisenhower Tunnel, 9 inches at Tower Snotel, 7 inches at Loveland Pass and 5 inches at Zirkel Snotel.  Peak wind gusts included:  88 mph at Georgetown, 85 mph atop Berthoud Pass, 81 mph at the NCAR Mesa Lab, 78 mph at the National Wind Technology Center, and 76 mph at Floyd Hill.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals included 9 inches at Tower Snotel and 5 inches at the Zirkel Snotel site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems brought an extended period of strong winds and snow to northern Park County.  As a result, the ensuing ground blizzards stranded residents and forced the closure of State Highway 285, as icy and snow-packed roads also caused several accidents.  In some places, snow drifts were reportedly 25 ft deep; trapping some residents in their homes for up to 10 days.  Fire and emergency personnel estimated up to 100 residents were blocked by snow in the communities of Hartsel and Jefferson.  Shelters were set up in Fairplay for stranded travelers and people evacuated from their homes.  A state of emergency was issued due to the inclimate weather conditions. The Department of Corrections sent prison inmates to Park County to help in snow removal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ground blizzards from a series of storm stranded residents for several days, and forced the closure of State Highway 285.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heavy and unstable snowpack produced an avalanche onto a snowmobile trail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three snowmobilers were coming down Little Box Canyon on a groomed trail in the southwestern Flat Tops north of the town of Rifle.  The first two riders noticed that the third was no longer behind them.|The pair turned around and headed up the canyon and found a fresh avalanche that had buried the third snowmobiler.  Rescuers retrieved the victim's body about three hours after the avalanche occurred.  The avalanche ran 300 feet down the steep slope and was 1.5 to 3 feet deep and 600 feet wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system produced isolated heavy snow amouts around the rim of the San Luis Valley, including the Crestone and Del Norte areas.  More widespread heavy snow fell in the eastern San Juan Mountains. Near the summit of Wolf Creek Pass, 13 inches of snow was recorded.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN EL PASO COUNTY / MONUMENT RIDGE / RAMPART RANGE BELOW 7500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system generated impressive snow amounts over northern El Paso County.  Isolated heavier snow amounts were also seen in the Walsenburg and Trinidad areas. Snow amounts in northern El Paso County totaled up to 21 inches in one part of the Black Forest...with more typical amounts from 10 to 15 inches in Palmer Lake...Monument...Air Force Academy...Black Forest...and Peyton areas.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN LAKE COUNTY / WESTERN MOSQUITO RANGE ABOVE 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving upper disturbance generated snow accumulations in excess of 8 inches across the higher terrain of the Mosquito Range, eastern Sawatch Range, and the La Garita and eastern San Juan Mountains.

A storm system brought moderate to heavy snowfall to areas in and near the Front Range Foothills, Urban Corridor and Palmer Divide.  Storm totals included:  10 inches, 4 miles south of Boulder; 8.5 inches, 3 miles southwest of Golden; 8 inches, 12 miles southeast of Aurora; 7 inches at Boulder, 1 mile east of Buckhorn Mountain, Fort Collins and Genessee.  A localized band of heavy snow also produced nearly 7 inches of snow in and around Greeley.

CZ_NAME: WEST JACKSON & WEST GRAND COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong upper level jet stream brought heavy snow and intense winds to parts of the North-Central Mountains.  The combination of  snow and wind produced whiteout conditions which resulted in several accidents and greatly increased the avalanche danger in the high country.  The dangerous road conditions forced the closure of Interstate 70, from the Eisenhower Tunnel to Silverthorne, until conditions improved.  The Red Cross opened a temporary shelter in Silverthorne for more than two hundred travelers.  Over a two-day period, Loveland ski area reported 43 inches of snow.  In addition, U.S. Highway 285, from Kenosha Pass to Fairplay, was closed due to blowing snow and icy roads.  ||Additional storm totals included:  34.5 inches at Fremont Pass Snotel, 27 inches at Grizzly Peak Snotel and St. Mary's Glacier, 24 inches at Rough and Tumble Snotel, 21 inches at Berthoud Summit Snotel,  19.5 inches at Jackwacker Gulch, Never Summer Snotels; 18 inches at Rabbit Ears Snotel, 16.5 inches at Lake Irene, Lynx Pass and Tower Snotels.  Peak wind reports included:  96 mph atop Berthoud Pass, 83 mph atop Niwot Ridge, and 68 mph atop Loveland Pass.
Strong winds developed in the foothills north of Interstate 70.  Peak wind reports included:  89 mph at Pinewood Lake, 83 mph, 6 miles northwest of Aspen Springs; and 80 mph near Jamestown.

An intense low pressure area developed over the Central Plains states.  Northwest winds of 45 to 70 mph developed in the strong surface pressure gradient west of the low pressure system which affected eastern Colorado, western Kansas and southwest Nebraska.  Several locations in northwest Kansas reported downed power lines and two vehicle rollovers were reported on Interstate 70 in Sherman county Kansas.

Yuma county Colorado received 6-10 inches of snow across the northern half of the county while Dundy county Nebraska picked up 6-10 inches across the northern 2/3 of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very intense winds developed across the Northeast Plains of Colorado following the passage of a storm system.  In Phillips County, damage was reported at Holyoke.  Extensive structural damage was observed at a local gas station, a city storage shed and a main street business.  One person was injured when attempting to clean up debris around the storage shed. A piece of tin flew off a nearby roof and struck the woman in the forehead.  She suffered a large cut that required several stitches.  ||In addition, a wind tower at the airport was knocked down.  Several homeowners in and around Holyoke reported damage.  Damage reports ranged from missing shingles and broken windows to trees falling onto houses.  Scattered power outages were reported throughout the area. ||Peak wind reports included:  78 mph, 4 miles east of Haxtun; 74 mph, 22 miles east of Hereford; 73 mph, 2 miles east of Amherst; 71 mph at Crook, 67 mph at Holyoke and Fort Morgan, 65 mph, 9 miles southwest of Sedgwick; 64 mph at Wiggins, 62 mph at Fleming, 61 mph at Akron, and 60 mph at Peetz.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong Chinook winds blasted areas in and near the Front Range Foothills of Larimer, Boulder and Jefferson Counties.  The wind blew down trees and power poles, downed electrical lines and fences, and damaged homes and vehicles.  Scattered power outages were reported all along the Front Range.  In Metropolitan Denver alone, 24,000 Xcel customers were affected by the outages.  Strong crosswinds also blew over some semi-trailers along Interstate 25, near the Wyoming state line.  In Larimer County, two small wildfires were sparked by downed power lines in Rist Canyon and near the Laporte/Bellvue areas.  Four  planes were damaged at the Vance Brand Municipal Airport in Longmont; one was wrecked.  Insurance companies estimated up to $7 million in damage along the Front Range and adjacent plains, making it the 4th costliest windstorm to hit Colorado.||Peak wind gusts included:  111 mph, 3 miles north of Masonville; 98 mph at Carter Lake; 87 mph at the National Wind Technology Center; 86 mph, 2 miles north of Longmont and at Pinewood Lake; 81 mph, 3 miles east of Gold Hill; 78 mph, 2 miles west-southwest of Broomfield; 77 mph at Erie; 76 mph, 21 miles north of New Raymer and 75 mph at Lafayette.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system brought heavy snow, very strong winds and whiteout conditions to the North-Central Mountains.  Storms totals generally ranged from 8 to 15 inches.  Peak wind gusts above timberline approached 100 mph.  The whiteout conditions forced the closure of several roadways, including the I-70 corridor between Vail and Clear Creek County. The Red Cross provided temporary shelters for approximately 160 travelers.||The increased avalanche danger also forced the closure of Berthoud, Loveland and Rabbit Ear's Passes.  Peak wind gusts included:  94 mph at Loveland Pass, 85 mph at Berthoud Pass, 74 mph atop Niwot Ridge, with 68 mph at Copper Mountain and Gunsight.  Storm totals included:  15 inches at the Eisenhower Tunnel, 12 inches at Breckenridge Ski Area, 11 inches at Tower Snotel and Winter Park Ski Area, 10 inches at St. Mary's Glacier, 9.5 inches at Berthoud Pass, 9 inches at Arapahoe Ridge and Lake Irene, with 8 inches at Fraser.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The snotel sites at Arapahoe Ridge and Lake Irene totaled 9 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong upper level jet stream brought heavy snow and intense winds to parts of the North-Central Mountains.  The combination of  snow and wind produced whiteout conditions which resulted in several accidents and greatly increased the avalanche danger in the high country.  The dangerous road conditions forced the closure of Interstate 70, from the Eisenhower Tunnel to Silverthorne, until conditions improved.  The Red Cross opened a temporary shelter in Silverthorne for more than two hundred travelers.  Over a two-day period, Loveland ski area reported 43 inches of snow.  In addition, U.S. Highway 285, from Kenosha Pass to Fairplay, was closed due to blowing snow and icy roads.  ||Additional storm totals included:  34.5 inches at Fremont Pass Snotel, 27 inches at Grizzly Peak Snotel and St. Mary's Glacier, 24 inches at Rough and Tumble Snotel, 21 inches at Berthoud Summit Snotel,  19.5 inches at Jackwacker Gulch, Never Summer Snotels; 18 inches at Rabbit Ears Snotel, 16.5 inches at Lake Irene, Lynx Pass and Tower Snotels.  Peak wind reports included:  96 mph atop Berthoud Pass, 83 mph atop Niwot Ridge, and 68 mph atop Loveland Pass.

This storm impacted all of western Colorado, resulting in widespread travel difficulties and many temporary road closures. Blizzard conditions occurred across much of the central and southern mountain areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frequent wind gusts of 50 to 75 mph produced widespread blowing and drifting snow, withd zero visibility at times. Snow drifts up to 10 feet deep were reported.  Many roads and highways were closed during the blizzard, including Highway 550 north of Durango and Highway 160 northeast of Pagosa Springs.  Measured peak wind gusts included 93 mph on Kendall Mountain and 100 mph on Eagle Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Highs winds struck parts of the eastern mountains and I-25 corridor in advance of a major winter storm. Two semi-trailers were overturned on I-25 on the north side of Colorado Springs, but there were no injuries.  Three semi-trailers were overturned south of Walsenburg in Huerfano County and in northern Las Animas County, north of Trinidad.  No injuries occurred.  There was some minor damage in Walsenburg, mainly to signs and the side of a building, where some bricks were dislodged. In Colorado Springs, a power line blew down on the west side, starting a blaze which destroyed a business.  Damage estimates with this non-severe wind in west-central Colorado Springs was $1 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storms moved across western Colorado bringing heavy snow and strong winds to the area.

A low pressure system brought 5-6 inches of snow to parts of the Tri-State area, accompanied by winds of 40-50 mph.  The combination of snow and wind resulted in jack-knifed trailers and several vehicle accidents. Some state and county roads were closed for a short period of time due to minor accidents, drifting and reduced visibility in falling and blowing snow. In Colorado, snow totals were:  Yuma county 4-6 inches, Kit Carson county 2-5 inches and in Cheyenne county 1-2 inches. Peak winds in all three counties  were around 45 mph for much of the day.

Lightning struck a home in Englewood during an electrically charged snowstorm.  A gas meter at the home was hit by lightning and burst into flames.  The unusual thunderstorm produced a combination of snow and graupel.  The graupel was up to one half inch in diameter.  Total snow accumulations ranged from 1 to 4 inches across the southern suburbs of Denver.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home during an electrically charged snowstorm. A gas meter at the home was hit by lightning and burst into flames.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN LAKE COUNTY / WESTERN MOSQUITO RANGE ABOVE 11000 FT

Wind whipped a small burn pile near Meeker into a wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind caused a small burn pile to get out of control and burn 500 acres of vegetation near Meeker.  Authorities evacuated several homes and an RV park.  One firefighter was taken to a nearby hospital after suffering from smoke inhalation while protecting a home.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system dropped heavy snow onto weak snow layers in the mountains which resulted in numerous avalanches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lone backcountry skier was caught and killed in an avalanche in the Richmond Ridge area, just south of Aspen Mountain. Sometime between 1 and 3 pm the victim exited the ski resort through a backcountry gate. Since he was traveling alone, the exact time of the incident is unknown. The victim was carried about 200 vertical feet down the slope before being buried in a group of trees.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system dropped heavy snow onto weak snow layers in the mountains which resulted in numerous avalanches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A skier was caught in a rare in-bounds avalanche on Vail Mountain. The avalanche was 100 to 150 feet wide and about 600 feet long. The skier was able to stay on top of the snow and in the end was buried waist deep in snow.  The victim's friend was able to dig him out, and the victim was not injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system dropped heavy snow onto weak snow layers in the mountains which resulted in numerous avalanches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A ski patroller was caught in an avalanche and carried into some trees in an area known as If Cliff at an elevation near 10,800 feet.  The victim was shaken but not injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snowboarder was killed by an avalanche.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sometime during the afternoon, a 36 year old male snowboarder was caught and killed in an avalanche approximately 2 miles northwest of Crested Butte. The soft slab avalanche occurred in an area known as Happy Chutes. The avalanche ran about 1,500 vertical feet, carrying the victim through a dense grove of trees, over a 20 to 30 foot cliff, then down a tight gully and finally to a stand of small trees. The victim was found about a week later under 3 to 4 feet of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system preceded by a deep and moist southwest flow brought a prolonged period of winter weather and widespread heavy snow to western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 10 to 25 inches occurred while frequent wind gusts of 30 to 60 mph produced blowing and drifting snow.  A peak wind gust of 91 mph was recorded on Eagle Mountain. Several avalanches occurred throughout the storm, some of which caused temporary road closures near Red Mountain Pass and Molas and Coalbank passes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist northwest flow produced significant snowfall and strong winds across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 12 inches occurred with locally higher amounts, including 17 inches at Red Mountain Pass. Wind gusts of 40 to 70 mph produced blowing and drifting snow. Electrical power was lost on Friday, November 27 for about 1,500 customers across portions of La Plata and Archuleta counties. The power outages were due to heavy wet snow that weighed down the lines and caused many power lines to snap.  See the November 2008 Storm Data publication for the beginning of this event.

An intense low pressure system produced widespread northwest winds of 60-70 mph across the Tri-State region. Light snowfall combined with the extreme wind, resulting in blizzard conditions in some areas. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire erupted west of Ordway and strong winds spread the blaze rapidly east northeastward.  Highways 96 and 71 were shut down, as 8900 acres of grassland was consumed. Twenty-four structures were destroyed west and north of Ordway. Over 150 power poles were destroyed.  The entire town of Ordway was evacuated.  Tragically, two firefighters perished when the truck they were in plummeted into a culvert, where a bridge had burned out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In Evergreen, a man suffered minor injuries when he was struck by lightning.  It entered his finger, traveled down his body, and exited his foot.  Heavy rain caused flash flooding over south Denver and its nearby suburbs.  Heavy rain, from 2.5 to 4 inches, fell in less than 90 minutes.  Firefighters rescued 20 people as water quickly rose along creeks, flooded roadways, and stranded motorists.  Three people had to be rescued along Cherry Creek when the bike path flooded.  In Morgan County, several county roads were washed out between Wiggins and Orchard.  Other roadways in the area were under water as up to 3 inches of rain reportedly fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive flooding was reported; with several motorists stranded in standing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought heavy snow to portions of the Front Range Foothills; and localized blizzard conditions to areas along the Palmer Divide.  In the foothills of Jefferson and Park Counties storm totals included:  16 inches at Genesee, 14 inches, 4 miles south of Evergreen; 13 inches, 3 miles southeast of Pinecliffe; 12 inches, 5 miles west-southwest of Conifer; 10 inches at Evergreen and 5 miles west of Littleton.  ||Along the Palmer Divide, south and southeast of Denver, gusty northerly winds and snow caused localized blizzards. Storm totals included:  7 inches, 2 miles east of Castle Rock, 6 inches, 4 miles east of Parker and 2 miles northwest of Elizabeth, and 5 inches near Castle Pines.  Winds gusts from 25 to 35 mph stirred up snow drifts from 1 to 3 feet in depth.

Storm totals included: 7 inches, 2 miles east of Castle Rock, 6 inches, 4 miles east of Parker and 2 miles northwest of Elizabeth, and 5 inches near Castle Pines. Winds gusts from 25 to 35 mph stirred up snow drifts from 1 to 3 feet in depth.

An intense low pressure system produced widespread northwest winds of 60-70 mph across the Tri-State region. Light snowfall of 1-2 inches, combined with the extreme wind, resulted in blizzard conditions across Yuma county, CO...Dundy county, NE and Cheyenne county, KS with visibility varying between zero and 1/4 mile in many of these areas throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of snow combined with wind gusts to 65 mph resulted in blizzard conditions for much of the day as visibilities dropped to under 1/8 mile in light snow and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist storm brought heavy snow to the North Central Mountains and Front Range Foothills of Colorado.  The storm spread into the Northeast Plains allowing for significant snowfall over parts of the the lower elevations as well. Strong wind gusts also accompanied the storm, with peak gusts ranging from 40 mph to around 50 mph.  Strong winds produced snow drifts up to 3 feet in depth.  ||Storm totals in the mountains and foothills included 15.5 inches at Joe Wright; 14.5 inches at Fremont Pass; 13.5 inches at Loveland Snotel; 13 inches at Gould; 12.5 inches at Aspen Springs and Buffalo Park Snotel; 11 inches, 4 miles west-northwest of Conifer; with 10.5 inches, 3 miles north of Central City and 6 miles southwest of Evergreen.  Lesser amounts of 5 to 9 inches were observed elsewhere.||Storm totals across the Northeast Plains included:  10 inches, 6 miles south of New Raymer, and 5 miles south of Sedgwick;  9.5 inches, 12 miles southeast of Aurora; 9 inches, 7 miles north of Paoli; 7 inches at Woodrow; 6.5 inches at Akron and 10 miles west-northwest of St. Petersburg; 5.5 inches at Fleming and 6 miles north of Hyde; with 5 inches, 13 miles southeast of Holyoke and near Peetz.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stotm totals ranged from 4.5 to 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm brought large hail and damaging winds to the Northeast Plains of Colorado.  Large hail up to one inch in diameter was observed in Washington County.  Strong thunderstorm winds to around 75 mph also caused damage.  In Logan County, trees and power lines were downed.  In Morgan County, one person was injured when a carport collapsed.  In Phillips County, an irrigation sprinkler was overturned, and a 3-ton grain feeder was moved from the pasture to a nearby barn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist storm brought heavy snow to the North Central Mountains and Front Range Foothills of Colorado.  The storm spread into the Northeast Plains allowing for significant snowfall over parts of the the lower elevations as well. Strong wind gusts also accompanied the storm, with peak gusts ranging from 40 mph to around 50 mph.  Strong winds produced snow drifts up to 3 feet in depth.  ||Storm totals in the mountains and foothills included 15.5 inches at Joe Wright; 14.5 inches at Fremont Pass; 13.5 inches at Loveland Snotel; 13 inches at Gould; 12.5 inches at Aspen Springs and Buffalo Park Snotel; 11 inches, 4 miles west-northwest of Conifer; with 10.5 inches, 3 miles north of Central City and 6 miles southwest of Evergreen.  Lesser amounts of 5 to 9 inches were observed elsewhere.||Storm totals across the Northeast Plains included:  10 inches, 6 miles south of New Raymer, and 5 miles south of Sedgwick;  9.5 inches, 12 miles southeast of Aurora; 9 inches, 7 miles north of Paoli; 7 inches at Woodrow; 6.5 inches at Akron and 10 miles west-northwest of St. Petersburg; 5.5 inches at Fleming and 6 miles north of Hyde; with 5 inches, 13 miles southeast of Holyoke and near Peetz.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  14.5 inches at Fremont Pass, 13.5 inches at Loveland Snotel and 9.5 inches at Jonas Pass.

Strong winds produced snow drifts up to 3 feet in depth.  ||Storm totals in the mountains and foothills included 15.5 inches at Joe Wright; 14.5 inches at Fremont Pass; 13.5 inches at Loveland Snotel; 13 inches at Gould; 12.5 inches at Aspen Springs and Buffalo Park Snotel; 11 inches, 4 miles west-northwest of Conifer; with 10.5 inches, 3 miles north of Central City and 6 miles southwest of Evergreen.  Lesser amounts of 5 to 9 inches were observed elsewhere.||Storm totals across the Northeast Plains included:  10 inches, 6 miles south of New Raymer, and 5 miles south of Sedgwick;  9.5 inches, 12 miles southeast of Aurora; 9 inches, 7 miles north of Paoli; 7 inches at Woodrow; 6.5 inches at Akron and 10 miles west-northwest of St. Petersburg; 5.5 inches at Fleming and 6 miles north of Hyde; with 5 inches, 13 miles southeast of Holyoke and near Peetz.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included 12.5 inches at Buffalo Park Snotel and 7.5 inches at Tower Snotel.

Snowmobilers triggered an avalanche in Rio Blanco County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Layers of weak and unstable snow followed by sustained, heavy snowfall, created avalanche prone conditions that gave way to a snowmobile induced avalanche during the afternoon hours of January 4. Three snowmobilers were riding up a snow-covered hill about six miles south of the Marapos Creek trailhead in Rio Blanco county south of Hamilton, when the avalanche occurred and buried one of the snowmobilers head first into the snow. The two other snowmobilers recovered the victim who was unconscious at the time, but regained consciousness shortly thereafter. He was taken to Memorial Hospital in Craig and then to Aurora Hospital.  The victim suffered some pulmonary problems from the snowmobile exhaust that filled his lungs with carbon monoxide while he was trapped underneath the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of disturbances within a moist southwest flow brought heavy snow and strong winds to much of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two feet of snow fell across the area while frequent wind gusts of 20 to 40 mph produced blowing and drifting snow. An accident involving 60 to 75 vehicles occurred in the westbound lanes of Interstate 70 at Mile Marker 189, about 1 mile west of the Vail Pass summit. The eastbound lanes were subsequently closed due to a combination of snow and blowing snow combined with poor road conditions. The interstate was closed for nearly 8 hours. One fatality (indirect) occurred in the accident and at least 22 people (indirect)were injured. Poor visibility combined with icy and snow packed roads caused drivers to collide with vehicles involved in the initial accident.

A powerful tornado swept north-northwestward across Weld County, carving a path of destruction, nearly 39 miles in length.  The tornado, up to one-mile wide at times, initially touched down northeast of Platteville and finally lifted 6 miles west-northwest of Wellington.  A tornado assessment in the aftermath of the tornado revealed extensive areas of damage.  On the enhanced Fujita Scale there were pockets of EF3 damage, mainly near the Missile Silo Park Campground, and to businesses and home in eastern Windsor.  Farmers also reported extensive damage to crops and irrigation equipment.  ||There was one fatality, and 78 injuries ranging from broken bones to minor cuts and lacerations.  One man was killed when he tried to escape a trailer park in his motor home.  Tractor trailers were flipped along U.S. Highway 85, and over 200 power poles were snapped or blown down. Approximately 60,000 people were left without electricity.  The tornado overturned 15 railroad cars and destroyed a lumber car on the Great Western Railway of Colorado.  The tornado also flattened Windsor's main feedlot and destroyed a dairy barn.  Most of the four-hundred cows were killed in the tornado or destroyed later. In addition to the damaging winds, the thunderstorm also produced hail up to the size of baseballs.  ||Another tornado rated an EF1, from a separate severe thunderstorm, briefly touched down near Dacono and destroyed 5 buildings. The tornado overturned a five-wheel trailer and injured the man who was sitting inside.  ||Preliminary estimates from FEMA indicated 850 homes were damaged, and nearly 300 homes were significantly damaged or destroyed.  The Poudre Valley Rural Electric Association reported $1 million of damage to electric transmission lines.  Privately insured damages totaled $147 million, making it the state's 4th costliest disaster.

CZ_NAME: LARIMER COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / NW WELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to the high country; with damaging winds and blizzard conditions to the Northeast Plains.  In the mountains storm totals ranged from 5 to 12 inches, with wind gusts to 86 mph recorded above timberline.  Across the Northeast Plains, peak wind gusts ranging from 60 to 70 mph damaged crops and property.  In addition to the wind, bands of moderate to heavy snow brought blizzard conditions to eastern Logan, Sedgwick, Phillips and northern Washington Counties. Snowfall totals in those counties ranged from 2 to 4 inches.||Multiple accidents occurred over Sedgwick County with whiteout conditions reported.  At least 40 vehicles slid of Interstate 76 and other area highways.  Three jack-knifed semis were also reported. Several roads and highways were closed across Phillips, Sedgwick and Washington Counties.  Some of the closures included:  State Highway 85, from Julesburg to Wray; U.S. Highway 6; and State Highway 23, from Holyoke to the Nebraska state line.  The dangerous weather conditions also forced the closure of all Sedgwick County schools.  Power outages were reported in Julesburg for much of the day.  On Interstate 70 near Watkins, localized whiteout conditions were partially to blame for a multi-car crash involving 14 vehicles.  One person was killed.   ||Further west, strong winds toppled a semi-trailer at the I-76 exit at Fort Morgan.  Trees and power lines were downed causing scattered electrical outages.  Wind damage to crops and property in Weld County was estimated to surpass the $400,000 mark.  The majority of the crops that were planted three weeks prior to this event, were blown away.||Peak wind gusts in the mountains included 86 mph atop Niwot Ridge, and 85 mph at Berthoud Pass.  Across the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains, peak wind gusts included:  71 mph at the Natural Fort Rest Area, 69 mph at Briggsdale, 67 mph at Sterling, 65 mph at Wiggins, 63 mph at Bennett and Loveland, 60 mph at Limon and Mead, 58 mph at Watkins and 54 mph at Denver International Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gusts to 73 mph was reported in Fort Collins.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds struck portions of the Urban Corridor.  At Carter Lake, strong winds damaged several boats in the north marina. A powerful tornado swept north-northwestward across Weld County, carving a path of destruction, nearly 39 miles in length.  The tornado, up to one-mile wide at times, initially touched down northeast of Platteville and finally lifted 6 miles west-northwest of Wellington.  A tornado assessment in the aftermath of the tornado revealed extensive areas of damage.  On the enhanced Fujita Scale there were pockets of EF3 damage, mainly near the Missile Silo Park Campground, and to businesses and home in eastern Windsor.  Farmers also reported extensive damage to crops and irrigation equipment.  ||There was one fatality, and 78 injuries ranging from broken bones to minor cuts and lacerations.  One man was killed when he tried to escape a trailer park in his motor home.  Tractor trailers were flipped along U.S. Highway 85, and over 200 power poles were snapped or blown down. Approximately 60,000 people were left without electricity.  The tornado overturned 15 railroad cars and destroyed a lumber car on the Great Western Railway of Colorado.  The tornado also flattened Windsor's main feedlot and destroyed a dairy barn.  Most of the four-hundred cows were killed in the tornado or destroyed later. In addition to the damaging winds, the thunderstorm also produced hail up to the size of baseballs.  ||Another tornado rated an EF1, from a separate severe thunderstorm, briefly touched down near Dacono and destroyed 5 buildings. The tornado overturned a five-wheel trailer and injured the man who was sitting inside.  ||Preliminary estimates from FEMA indicated 850 homes were damaged, and nearly 300 homes were significantly damaged or destroyed.  The Poudre Valley Rural Electric Association reported $1 million of damage to electric transmission lines.  Privately insured damages totaled $147 million, making it the state's 4th costliest disaster.

A large area of rain and embedded thunderstorms moved slowly across extreme eastern Colorado into western Kansas during the overnight hours. Rainfall reports of 4-6 inches were common across Yuma, Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties in Colorado.  Many county roads were closed or impassible in Yuma county for 24 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous county roads were closed for 24 hours due to high water associated with very heavy rainfall overnight. Rainfall reports of 4-6 inches were common, especially across SE Yuma county from Bonny Lake and Hale west to Kirk and north to Idalia. The entrance to Bonny Park was closed due to high water. Vernon reported 6.5 inches of rain, 5.5 inches fell 5 miles NNE of Hale, with 4 inches reported 10 miles east of Vernon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of rain and embedded thunderstorms moved slowly across extreme eastern Colorado into western Kansas during the overnight hours. Rainfall reports of 4-6 inches were common across Yuma, Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties in Colorado.  Many county roads were closed or impassible in Yuma county for 24 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Road W was impassible due to water nearly a foot deep between Rd 23 and Rd 24.  The cooperative observer nearby recorded 4.2 inches of rainfall overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system moved across western Colorado and produced heavy snow and strong winds across the mountains. Significant snowfall also impacted many valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 15 to 32 inches blanketed the area.  A locally heavier amount of 40 inches was measured at the avalanche monument along Highway 550. Frequent wind gusts of 25 to 50 mph produced blowing and drifting snow. A wind gust of 60 mph was recorded at Mount Abrahms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system moved across western Colorado and produced heavy snow and strong winds across the mountains. Significant snowfall also impacted many valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 10 to 30 inches occurred while frequent wind gusts of 25 to 50 mph produced blowing and drifting snow. A wind gust of 60 mph was recorded at Mount Putney.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system moved across western Colorado and produced heavy snow and strong winds across the mountains. Significant snowfall also impacted many valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 6 inches occurred above 7500 feet while wind gusts of 25 to 40 mph caused blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system moved across western Colorado and produced heavy snow and strong winds across the mountains. Significant snowfall also impacted many valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall of 5 to 12 inches blanketed the area.  Locally heavier amounts included 16 inches at Oak Creek and 13 inches at Yampa. Wind gusts of 20 to 30 mph caused blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system moved across western Colorado and produced heavy snow and strong winds across the mountains. Significant snowfall also impacted many valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 3 to 6 inches occurred across half of the area, mainly in the Cattle Creek, Wolcott, and Basalt areas. Wind gusts of 20 to 30 mph caused blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures caused rapid melting of mountain snowpack and high flows in area rivers, creeks, and streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rivers, small streams, and creeks across much of Mesa County exceeded bankfull and caused minor flooding. Emergency Manager and weather spotter reports indicated low land flooding along portions of the Colorado River from DeBeque Canyon to the Utah State Line. The Interstate 70 median east of the Loma exit was flooded. Water lapped at the side of the westbound Interstate 70 bridge near the Loma Exit causing officials to close one lane in each direction for a period. Water covered a section of the westbound interstate lanes in DeBeque Canyon and near the Highway 65 cutoff. The Colorado River was out of its banks on both sides of the river in the Highway 340 Bridge area and encroached into an adjacent State Park campground. Portions of the Colorado Riverfront Trail in Grand Junction were closed due to high water. The Gunnison River was out of its banks and covered an access road to a construction company in the vicinity of the Department of Energy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred in Arapahoe and Weld Counties.  A peak wind gusts to 60 mph was reported in Centenniel.  Near Frederick, strong winds snapped 3 power poles and caused a small grassfire near State Highway 52 and Interstate 25.
A severe thunderstorm produced large hail, up to 2 inches in diameter, in Logan County.  In addition, a small landspout tornado touched down near Cope, in southeast Washington County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado touched down but did no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail and intense winds in Phillips County, near Holyoke.  Thunderstorm winds downed several power poles along State Highway 385, north of Holyoke, and overturned irrigation sprinklers.  An elm tree was knocked down in Holyoke. Farms located southeast of Holyoke, near the state line, were also heavily damaged.  At one farm, the strong winds knocked over two grain bins and damaged three sprinklers.  Another farmer lost 1000 acres of corn due to hail.

CZ_NAME: SE ELBERT COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / SOUTH LINCOLN COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to the high country; with damaging winds and blizzard conditions to the Northeast Plains.  In the mountains storm totals ranged from 5 to 12 inches, with wind gusts to 86 mph recorded above timberline.  Across the Northeast Plains, peak wind gusts ranging from 60 to 70 mph damaged crops and property.  In addition to the wind, bands of moderate to heavy snow brought blizzard conditions to eastern Logan, Sedgwick, Phillips and northern Washington Counties. Snowfall totals in those counties ranged from 2 to 4 inches.||Multiple accidents occurred over Sedgwick County with whiteout conditions reported.  At least 40 vehicles slid of Interstate 76 and other area highways.  Three jack-knifed semis were also reported. Several roads and highways were closed across Phillips, Sedgwick and Washington Counties.  Some of the closures included:  State Highway 85, from Julesburg to Wray; U.S. Highway 6; and State Highway 23, from Holyoke to the Nebraska state line.  The dangerous weather conditions also forced the closure of all Sedgwick County schools.  Power outages were reported in Julesburg for much of the day.  On Interstate 70 near Watkins, localized whiteout conditions were partially to blame for a multi-car crash involving 14 vehicles.  One person was killed.   ||Further west, strong winds toppled a semi-trailer at the I-76 exit at Fort Morgan.  Trees and power lines were downed causing scattered electrical outages.  Wind damage to crops and property in Weld County was estimated to surpass the $400,000 mark.  The majority of the crops that were planted three weeks prior to this event, were blown away.||Peak wind gusts in the mountains included 86 mph atop Niwot Ridge, and 85 mph at Berthoud Pass.  Across the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains, peak wind gusts included:  71 mph at the Natural Fort Rest Area, 69 mph at Briggsdale, 67 mph at Sterling, 65 mph at Wiggins, 63 mph at Bennett and Loveland, 60 mph at Limon and Mead, 58 mph at Watkins and 54 mph at Denver International Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust to 58 mph was observed in Karval.

Very heavy rainfall occurred during the middle to late afternoon hours, resulting in rapid runoff and low land and county road flooding over extreme north central Kit Carson and extreme southern Yuma counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported 5.35 inches of rain in 90 minutes which resulted in low land flooding, numerous flooded basements and county roads becoming impassible in northern Kit Carson and southern Yuma counties. This precipitation fell on already saturated soils after a week of heavy rain episodes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked a fire east of Guffey, in Park County.  The combination of dry conditions and gusty winds allowed the fire to spread.  It eventually consumed 1,115 acres. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado touched down but did no damage.

Severe thunderstorms produced large hail up to the size of ping pong balls in the Arvada area, damaging automobiles.  As the severe thunderstorms developed across the Northeast Plains, large hail, damaging winds and tornadoes were reported.  Most of the wind damage occurred in Washington County, south of Akron.  Strong thunderstorm winds reportedly blew down sheds and snapped six power poles; and a roof was partially taken off one house.  Silos were also destroyed by the wind.  Several cars in Otis had their windows shattered by hail.  The windows of houses and buildings in the areas were also broken.  Heavy rain associated with the storms also caused flash flooding in Morgan and northern Washington Counties.  County officials reported 9 to 24 inches of water over many roadways in and around Fort Morgan and Brush.  Heavy rain caused flooding along State Highway 71 south of Brush.  Many farm fields were completely covered in water as Little Beaver Creek and numerous irrigation ditches had jumped their banks.  A storm spotter near the intersection of County Roads 14 and P measured over 5 inches of rainfall.

A warm spell caused rapid melting of mountain snowpack which produced high flows in creeks and streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt runoff caused some streams and creeks to exceed bankfull resulting in minor flooding. A seasonal stream came out of its banks and flooded the basement of a home south of Crested Butte and threatened a couple other homes. Heavy equipment was used to build some dikes and divert the water. Several homes were surrounded by water near the confluence of the Slate River and East River, which is about 7 miles southeast of Crested Butte and 10 miles north of Almont. The dirt roads leading to those residences were under 3 to 6 inches of water. Additionally, the East River overflowed at a few other locations which flooded pasture land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm spell caused rapid melting of mountain snowpack that resulted in high flows on streams and creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt runoff caused some streams and creeks to exceed bankfull. Culverts were overwhelmed in areas and portions of roads were damaged. In some cases, minor flooding and road damage occurred due to snowmelt water that cascaded off slopes above roads. Water 6 to 8 inches deep flowed across some roads. The force of the water caused a section of road between the Elk River Guest Ranch and Vista Verde Guest Ranch to slough off. The damaged road section was about 20 feet wide and plunged 12 to 14 feet down.

A late season storm system brought cold and unstable air to the area resulting in heavy snow and strong winds across the mountains of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall of 8 to 20 inches occurred with a local amount of 2 feet at the Columbus Basin SNOTEL. Wind gusts of 20 to 30 mph produced blowing and drifting snow. Numerous natural and human caused avalanches occurred. The Iron Horse Bicycle Classic road race and tour was cancelled due to the snow. This was the first time in its 37 year history that the Durango to Silverton race was cancelled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season storm system brought cold and unstable air to the area resulting in heavy snow and strong winds across the mountains of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall of 5 to 13 inches occurred. Wind gusts of 20 to 40 mph produced blowing and drifting snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 10 inches occurred above 8000 feet while wind gusts of 25 to 45 mph produced blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wild fire erupted in rugged, remote terrain on the Army's Pinion Canyon Maneuver Site in north central Las Animas County.  It eventually scorched around 45,000 acres.  No structures were damaged or destroyed.  The Purgatoire River acted as a nature fire break to the south and east.  Approximately 125 firefighters from Fort Carson and the USFS helped to contain the blaze.  On the 19th...thunderstorms with heavy rain overspread the area...helping to speed containment.  The cost of fighting the fire came to $2.8 million.

Strong thunderstorm winds, producing gusts from 60 mph to 80 mph, caused tree damage near Akron and Peetz.  The fairgrounds near Akron was also hit hard.  Several awnings were bent or blown down and a tent collapsed.  There were numerous reports of broken car windows and dents from fallen and blowing debris.  The strong winds also downed power lines.  West of Akron, three cows and two calves were electrocuted by the downed lines.

Monsoonal moisture was responsible for thunderstorms, including a few which produced heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on the mountains adjacent to the southeast part of the town of Ouray produced flash flooding on Oak Creek and Canyon Creek.  The wall of water was six feet deep at the Box Canyon geothermal water plant where sediment got into the water line.  Sediment carried by the  water line was in inch deep at the Ouray Hot Springs.  The Hot Springs had to be closed around 4 PM MDT and remained closed for the rest of that day to clean out the sediment.  Six feet of South Pinecrest Road was washed out, and a culvert under that road was damaged.  Debris up to a foot-and-a-half deep was deposited on other roads.

Lightning sparked a grassfire that consumed 300 acres on the northern edge of Green Mountain, in Jefferson County.  Gusty winds and very dry conditions allowed the fire to quickly spread and threaten several houses.  Fortunately, there was only minor damage to homes due to smoke and melted siding.  A severe thunderstorm also produced nickel size hail near Peetz, in Logan County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced lightning strikes and power outages in La Plata and Archuleta counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest flow transported deep subtropical moisture across western Colorado as an upper level moved east across the Great Basin. This system was quickly followed by a strong cold front out of the northwest. The combination of the two systems brought heavy snow and strong winds to western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three feet of snowfall blanketed the area with local amounts of nearly 4 feet around Silverton Ski Area. Frequent wind gusts of 40 to 80 mph buffeted the area. Wind gusts of 102 mph and 100 mph were recorded at Putney Mountain and Eagle Mountain. Highway 550 over Red Mountain Pass, Molas Pass, and Coalbank Pass was closed. Highway 145 over Lizard Head Pass and Highway 160 over Wolf Creek Pass were closed as well due to the adverse conditions and snow slides.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful tornado swept north-northwestward across Weld County, carving a path of destruction, nearly 39 miles in length.  The tornado, up to one-mile wide at times, initially touched down northeast of Platteville and finally lifted 6 miles west-northwest of Wellington.  A tornado assessment in the aftermath of the tornado revealed extensive areas of damage.  On the enhanced Fujita Scale there were pockets of EF3 damage, mainly near the Missile Silo Park Campground, and to businesses and home in eastern Windsor.  Farmers also reported extensive damage to crops and irrigation equipment.  ||There was one fatality, and 78 injuries ranging from broken bones to minor cuts and lacerations.  One man was killed when he tried to escape a trailer park in his motor home.  Tractor trailers were flipped along U.S. Highway 85, and over 200 power poles were snapped or blown down. Approximately 60,000 people were left without electricity.  The tornado overturned 15 railroad cars and destroyed a lumber car on the Great Western Railway of Colorado.  The tornado also flattened Windsor's main feedlot and destroyed a dairy barn.  Most of the four-hundred cows were killed in the tornado or destroyed later. In addition to the damaging winds, the thunderstorm also produced hail up to the size of baseballs.  ||Another tornado rated an EF1, from a separate severe thunderstorm, briefly touched down near Dacono and destroyed 5 buildings. The tornado overturned a five-wheel trailer and injured the man who was sitting inside.  ||Preliminary estimates from FEMA indicated 850 homes were damaged, and nearly 300 homes were significantly damaged or destroyed.  The Poudre Valley Rural Electric Association reported $1 million of damage to electric transmission lines.  Privately insured damages totaled $147 million, making it the state's 4th costliest disaster.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful tornado continued into Larimer County.  A barn and two out buildings were destroyed near Timnath.  A center pivot was overturned and a parked car was swept around the corner of a house and rolled over. The tornado finally lifted 6 miles west-northwest of Wellington.

CZ_NAME: COLORADO SPRINGS VICINITY / SOUTHERN EL PASO COUNTY / RAMPART RANGE BELOW 7500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked a wildfire which scorched around 8500 acres east of Fountain.  The fire began south of the intersection of Squirrel Creek Road and Milne Road and spread to the north and west.  Seventeen structures were destroyed, including three houses.  One house was designated a Centennial Ranch by the Colorado Historical Society.  The blaze was contained seven hours after it began. Over 60 firefighters fought the blaze.

A severe thunderstorm struck southern Saguache County, northern Rio Grande County and northern Alamosa County in the San Luis Valley. This may have been the worst hail storm in the valley in nearly 30 years. Hail...ranging in size from quarters to tennis balls...did extensive crop and property damage.  Around 28,000 acres of crop land was affected with crop losses estimated over $42 million.  Around 9,000 acres of the 57,000 acres of potatoes in the valley were damaged...with estimated losses over $32 million.  Around 12,000 acres of wheat and barley were damaged...with estimated losses at over $9 million.

In Evergreen, a man suffered minor injuries when he was struck by lightning.  It entered his finger, traveled down his body, and exited his foot.  Heavy rain caused flash flooding over south Denver and its nearby suburbs.  Heavy rain, from 2.5 to 4 inches, fell in less than 90 minutes.  Firefighters rescued 20 people as water quickly rose along creeks, flooded roadways, and stranded motorists.  Three people had to be rescued along Cherry Creek when the bike path flooded.  In Morgan County, several county roads were washed out between Wiggins and Orchard.  Other roadways in the area were under water as up to 3 inches of rain reportedly fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Greeley.  Many roads in and around town were either flooded or washed out.  Floodwaters along 10th Street, the main thoroughfare in Greeley, had anywhere from 1 to 3 feet of water covering the roadway.  Several cars were stalled in the floodwaters.  Extensive flooding was also observed in Evans, along U.S. Highways 34 and 85.  The rain flooded the basement of Greeley City Hall along with numerous other homes and businesses in town.  In addition, heavy rain caused considerable damage to several county roads and culverts south of Akron, in Washington County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The effects of long term drought continued to impact extreme northwest Colorado in early April but diminished by months end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate Drought conditions existed across extreme northwest Moffat County at the start of the month. Significant winter precipitation and cool spring temperatures resulted in the dissipation of drought conditions by the end of April aross northwest Moffat County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire spread along the banks of the Roaring Fork River near Carbondale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire of unknown origins started about 1.2 miles east of Carbondale along County Road 100.  Wind gusts to 50 mph quickly spread the fire to over 1000 acres, and the fire jumped across the Roaring Fork River in the process. Four houses caught on fire, though efforts by firefighters prevented the houses from being destroyed. Many lawns and trees were charred.  A man received burns to the back of his head and arms as he was fishing along the Roaring Fork River.  County Road 100 was closed for an extensive period of time, while Highway 82 was closed for three hours.  Hundreds of residents and a large number of livestock in the area were evacuated.  There were 140 firefighters who fought the blaze, along with nearly as many volunteers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and strong winds resulted in several avalanches on Red Mountain Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-one natural avalanches on Red Mountain Pass hit the road and caused periods of road closure. Some of the avalanches were up to 8 feet deep and several hundred feet wide on the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain totaling over four inches in localized areas on the east side of Colorado Springs.  Some roads were closed due to high water, and a bridge over Sand Creek was compromised.  Fountain Creek briefly went over flood stage in the Fountain area.

Several landspout tornadoes developed along a boundary to the southeast of the Denver metropolitan area.  In northwest Elbert County, minor damage was reported.  The damage consisted of downed power lines, broken windows and a damaged out building. Severe thunderstorms also produced very heavy rain and hail, up to the size of quarters.  ||In Douglas County, heavy rain caused flash flooding near the town of Westcreek.  Private drives to residences in the Trail Creek subdivision were washed out.  A mudslide closed State Highway 67, near Rainbow Falls Road.  The road in the YMCA Camp Shady Brook was also washed out and bridges were damaged.  In the camp, the gullies carved by the floodwaters were 6 feet wide by 5 feet deep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout tornado touched down briefly.  Minor damage was reported which consisted of downed power lines, broken windows and a damaged out building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home and caused extensive fire damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in Vail Valley and ignited a fire within the floor structure and the ceiling cavity. The fire burned through five structural floor joists.  The lightning also blew apart a section of the stone chimney and sent pieces flying up to 20 feet away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large hail fell in a small area of northern Routt County.

In Park County at 11-Mile Reservoir, a waterspout caused damage when it transitioned into a landspout.  Five vehicles were damaged, including a motor home and a pickup truck with a camper that overturned.  One camp trailer was completely destroyed.  Several people suffered superficial injuries which consisted of minor cuts and scrapes.  In Douglas County near Westcreek, another landspout touched down.  One man was seriously injured when he tried to escape several falling trees in his ATV.  One of the trees struck his back and broke two vertabrae.  Another camper narrowly escaped injury.  Seconds after he backed up his truck, a tree came down where his vehicle had been parked.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout transitioned to a landspout and caused damage to several recreational vehicles.  Several peopled suffered minor injuries from flying debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture produced thunderstorms, some of which produced heavy rainfall in Ouray County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood came down Corbett Creek leaving a debris fan several hundred yards long, 300 feet wide and five feet deep.  A house on Whispering Pines Drive sustained extensive damage to the foundation and exterior, with the deck having been ripped off.  Another house on Chipmunk Way had debris up to a half foot deep into the basement and garage, and a deposit of debris up to 2 feet deep on the deck and against the house. A family of hikers on the Dallas Trail became stranded when their parked car was washed away.  Their car was a total loss.  Another car was carried away from a driveway and dropped over a short cliff. A boulder the size of a small car fell onto nearby Highway 550.  County Road 17 was washed out and another nearby private road was washed out in places. The flash flood resulted in Corbett Creek carving out a new channel.  A local official said this was the worst flash flood he had seen in that area since 1991.

Extensive crop damage was reported near Wellington.  Other severe thunderstorms developed southeast of Denver, where hail up to the size of ping pong balls was reported near Agate.

Severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding across parts of Larimer, Lincoln, Morgan and Washington Counties.  Strong thunderstorm wind gusts to 83 mph were reported in Morgan County, near Brush.  Several small trees and power lines were downed.  The wind downed a power pole during a rodeo, forcing the evacuation of the fairgrounds for the first time in its history.  Large hail up to the size of quarters was also observed.  Rainfall of 2.61 inches was measured just west of Brush, most of which fell in less than 45 minutes.  ||In addition, heavy rain caused flash flooding along and south of a line extending from Brush to around Midway, west of Akron.  Floodwaters up to 1 foot in depth inundated streets and yards around Brush.  Some houses, small businesses, and the hospital in Brush were flooded. Several patients had to be moved to the inner hallways of the building.  High water forced the closure of State Highway 34, between Brush and Akron.  Heavy rain also caused flash flooding over northern Lincoln County.  State Highway 71, north of Limon, was covered in 2 to 3 feet of water; and several county roads were flooded.

A powerful tornado swept north-northwestward across Weld County, carving a path of destruction, nearly 39 miles in length.  The tornado, up to one-mile wide at times, initially touched down northeast of Platteville and finally lifted 6 miles west-northwest of Wellington.  A tornado assessment in the aftermath of the tornado revealed extensive areas of damage.  On the enhanced Fujita Scale there were pockets of EF3 damage, mainly near the Missile Silo Park Campground, and to businesses and home in eastern Windsor.  Farmers also reported extensive damage to crops and irrigation equipment.  ||There was one fatality, and 78 injuries ranging from broken bones to minor cuts and lacerations.  One man was killed when he tried to escape a trailer park in his motor home.  Tractor trailers were flipped along U.S. Highway 85, and over 200 power poles were snapped or blown down. Approximately 60,000 people were left without electricity.  The tornado overturned 15 railroad cars and destroyed a lumber car on the Great Western Railway of Colorado.  The tornado also flattened Windsor's main feedlot and destroyed a dairy barn.  Most of the four-hundred cows were killed in the tornado or destroyed later. In addition to the damaging winds, the thunderstorm also produced hail up to the size of baseballs.  ||Another tornado rated an EF1, from a separate severe thunderstorm, briefly touched down near Dacono and destroyed 5 buildings. The tornado overturned a five-wheel trailer and injured the man who was sitting inside.  ||Preliminary estimates from FEMA indicated 850 homes were damaged, and nearly 300 homes were significantly damaged or destroyed.  The Poudre Valley Rural Electric Association reported $1 million of damage to electric transmission lines.  Privately insured damages totaled $147 million, making it the state's 4th costliest disaster.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful tornado swept north-northwestward across Weld County and into Larimer County, carving a path of destruction, nearly 39 miles in length. The tornado, up to one-mile wide at times, initially touched down northeast of Platteville and finally lifted 6 miles west-northwest of Wellington.  A tornado assessment in the aftermath of the tornado revealed extensive areas of damage. On the enhanced Fujita Scale there were pockets of EF3 damage, mainly near the Missile Silo Park Campground, and to businesses and home in eastern Windsor. ||There was one fatality, and 78 injuries. One man was killed when he tried to escape the trailer park in his motor home.  Preliminary estimates from FEMA indicated 850 homes were damaged, and nearly 300 homes were significantly damaged or destroyed. Privately insured damages totaled $147 million, and the Poudre Valley Rural Electric Associated reported $1 million of damage to electric transmission lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure area developed over the Central Plains states.  Northwest winds of 45 to 70 mph developed in the strong surface pressure gradient west of the low pressure system which affected eastern Colorado, western Kansas and southwest Nebraska.  Several locations in northwest Kansas reported downed power lines and two vehicle rollovers were reported on Interstate 70 in Sherman county Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Newspaper in Wray also reported a large tree was blown over on Lincoln Street which downed power lines and caused a power outage in the west portion of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe storms occurred...the most severe over Pueblo West...where hail the size of baseballs occurred.

Severe thunderstorm brought large hail and damaging winds to the Northeast Plains of Colorado.  Large hail up to one inch in diameter was observed in Washington County.  Strong thunderstorm winds to around 75 mph also caused damage.  In Logan County, trees and power lines were downed.  In Morgan County, one person was injured when a carport collapsed.  In Phillips County, an irrigation sprinkler was overturned, and a 3-ton grain feeder was moved from the pasture to a nearby barn.
Isolated thunderstorms developed over eastern Colorado and produced generally quarter to half dollar size hail though a few reports of golf ball size hail were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of penny to quarter size hail relayed by the NSSL SHAVE project.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail up to the size of ping pong balls in the Arvada area, damaging automobiles.  As the severe thunderstorms developed across the Northeast Plains, large hail, damaging winds and tornadoes were reported.  Most of the wind damage occurred in Washington County, south of Akron.  Strong thunderstorm winds reportedly blew down sheds and snapped six power poles; and a roof was partially taken off one house.  Silos were also destroyed by the wind.  Several cars in Otis had their windows shattered by hail.  The windows of houses and buildings in the areas were also broken.  Heavy rain associated with the storms also caused flash flooding in Morgan and northern Washington Counties.  County officials reported 9 to 24 inches of water over many roadways in and around Fort Morgan and Brush.  Heavy rain caused flooding along State Highway 71 south of Brush.  Many farm fields were completely covered in water as Little Beaver Creek and numerous irrigation ditches had jumped their banks.  A storm spotter near the intersection of County Roads 14 and P measured over 5 inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado touched down but did no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours across eastern Colorado, resulting in the first severe thunderstorm events of 2008 in the Goodland County Warning Area. Hail diameters of nickel to golf ball size were reported.
Thunderstorms produced hail up to the size of nickels in Custer and Huerfano Counties.
The remnants of Hurricane Dolly drifted across eastern Colorado and western Kansas, producing locally heavy rainfall and a few isolated severe wind and hail reports along the Colorado-Kansas border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This report was received from the NSSL SHAVE project.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms occurred...one producing a funnel cloud over Fort Carson south of Colorado Springs.
Severe thunderstorm produced hail up to the size of quarters in Logan and Washington Counties.  A few weak tornadoes also touched down but did no damage.
Severe thunderstorms producing hail up to the size of quarters and wind gusts around 60 mph at times from El Paso County to Bent County.

A series of severe thunderstorms developed in a SW-NE line and moved northeast, producing repeated heavy rain and hail episodes from northern Yuma County Colorado eastward across Dundy and Hitchcock counties in Nebraska. Several flash flood reports, and dozens of large hail reports were received.  Several brief tornadoes were also reported, but no damage was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from two separate storms on June 4th resulted in continued minor flooding in town overnight and the following morning with some roads remaining impassible. All reservoirs and ditches remained at capacity during the morning of June 5th before the water slowly receded after the reservoirs were pumped out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across Yuma and Kit Carson counties, producing several severe hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observered reported quarter to half dollar sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Five children and one adult were injured when winds, from a dry microburst, picked up an inflateable play house at Larimer County Reservoir.  The wind swept up the house from its location on the beach, and knocked it into a steel pole before it came to a stop.  One child was airlifted to Loveland with serious injuries, while the others suffered minor to moderate injuries.  In Denver, strong thunderstorm winds blew several trees down, damaging homes and downing power lines.  An elderly man was killed when a wind damaged tree broke free and crushed him while he attempted to remove it.  The downed power lines caused outages to about 500 Xcel Energy customers.

Severe thunderstorms produced large hail in parts of Douglas and Weld Counties.  Some car damage was reported in Douglas County, northeast of Parker.  Large hail, up to the size of golf balls, was reported near Briggsdale.

Severe thunderstorms developed over Logan and Washington Counties.  The storms produced wind gusts to 60 mph and large hail, up to the size of quarters.  In Aurora, an unoccupied home was struck by lightning causing extensive roof damage.

Severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding across parts of Larimer, Lincoln, Morgan and Washington Counties.  Strong thunderstorm wind gusts to 83 mph were reported in Morgan County, near Brush.  Several small trees and power lines were downed.  The wind downed a power pole during a rodeo, forcing the evacuation of the fairgrounds for the first time in its history.  Large hail up to the size of quarters was also observed.  Rainfall of 2.61 inches was measured just west of Brush, most of which fell in less than 45 minutes.  ||In addition, heavy rain caused flash flooding along and south of a line extending from Brush to around Midway, west of Akron.  Floodwaters up to 1 foot in depth inundated streets and yards around Brush.  Some houses, small businesses, and the hospital in Brush were flooded. Several patients had to be moved to the inner hallways of the building.  High water forced the closure of State Highway 34, between Brush and Akron.  Heavy rain also caused flash flooding over northern Lincoln County.  State Highway 71, north of Limon, was covered in 2 to 3 feet of water; and several county roads were flooded.
A severe storm produced hail up to the size of golfballs.
